text:
HEAD AND NECK ONCOLOGY SURVIVORSHIP CLINIC
OUTPATIENT FOLLOW-UP NOTE

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: Head and Neck Medical Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Routine Survivorship Follow-Up (30-Month Post-Treatment)

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 54-year-old never-smoking West African woman with a history of HPV-positive right base of tongue squamous cell carcinoma (T2N1M0, p16-positive, stage I by HPV-positive oropharyngeal staging criteria) who presents today for her 30-month post-chemoradiation survivorship visit. She has no new complaints and continues to do well.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu completed definitive concurrent chemoradiation approximately 30 months ago. She received IMRT to the oropharynx and bilateral neck (70 Gy in 35 fractions to the high-risk volume) with concurrent weekly cisplatin (40 mg/m2, 6 of 7 cycles delivered, cumulative dose approximately 414 mg/m2 given a one-week hold during week 5 for grade 2 nephrotoxicity). Her treatment course was complicated by grade 3 mucositis requiring hospitalization and initiation of PEG tube feeds, as well as grade 2 radiation dermatitis, grade 2 dysgeusia, grade 2 xerostomia, and transient nephrotoxicity. She achieved a complete metabolic and pathologic response, confirmed on restaging PET-CT at 10 weeks post-treatment and on direct laryngoscopy with biopsy at approximately 5 months post-treatment. She has had no evidence of disease at all subsequent imaging and clinical evaluations, including PET-CT at 9 months, MRI neck at 18 months, and PET-CT at 24 months post-treatment. She reached the important 2-year NED milestone at her last visit, at which point surveillance was de-escalated to annual imaging and clinical exams every 6 months.

Today she presents for her 30-month follow-up. She reports no new head and neck symptoms. She denies any new neck masses, oral pain, dysphagia worsening, odynophagia, or otalgia. No new voice changes beyond the mild hoarseness she has carried since completing radiation. She denies any epistaxis, hemoptysis, or nasal obstruction. She reports no symptoms suggestive of distant metastatic disease -- specifically no bone pain, no neurological symptoms, no new pulmonary complaints, and no constitutional B symptoms such as unintentional weight loss, drenching night sweats, or fevers. She has returned to working full-time as a high school music teacher and describes her quality of life as excellent. She continues to manage her late radiation toxicities well.

Regarding known late effects:

Xerostomia: She reports this has continued to gradually improve. At her 24-month visit she was describing grade 1 xerostomia, and she feels this is unchanged or marginally better. She uses Biotene gel at bedtime and keeps water available throughout the day and while teaching. She is tolerating pilocarpine 5 mg twice daily (dose was tapered from three times daily at 18 months), and feels it provides modest relief without intolerable cholinergic side effects.

Swallowing: She is eating a regular diet with only infrequent avoidance of particularly dry or dense-textured foods such as crackers and crusty bread. She has not had any episodes of aspiration or coughing during meals. She no longer requires speech-language pathology visits but continues her home swallowing exercise program.

Trismus: Mouth opening has been gradually improving with TheraBite exercises. She continues these daily. At her 18-month visit, mouth opening was 4.1 cm; she reports no subjective worsening.

Hearing: She is aware of mild high-frequency hearing loss but says it is not limiting her professionally or socially. She has not required hearing aids. She is scheduled for annual audiology follow-up.

Hypothyroidism: She is tolerating levothyroxine 100 mcg daily well. TSH has been normal at recent checks.

Cardiovascular risk: She is newly started on atorvastatin 10 mg daily as discussed below, and is tolerating it without myalgias or GI side effects.

Genetics: She followed up with genetics and was counseled that her BRCA2 p.D2723H variant has been reclassified as likely benign. She found this reassuring and is not pursuing additional cascade testing of family members at this time.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Positive for: Persistent mild xerostomia (grade 1, stable), mild high-frequency hearing loss (stable, grade 1), occasional avoidance of dry foods with meals.

Negative for: New neck masses, new dysphagia, odynophagia, hemoptysis, otalgia, hoarseness worsening, epistaxis, nasal obstruction, facial numbness, shoulder weakness, trismus worsening, neck pain, bone pain, chest pain, shortness of breath, hemoptysis, neurologic symptoms, unintentional weight loss, fevers, night sweats. No myalgias on statin. No cholinergic side effects from pilocarpine beyond mild perspiration. No palpitations. No symptoms of hypothyroidism.

---

MEDICATIONS

1. Levothyroxine 100 mcg orally daily (radiation-induced hypothyroidism)
2. Pilocarpine 5 mg orally twice daily (radiation-induced xerostomia)
3. Atorvastatin 10 mg orally daily (started today per cardiovascular risk reduction, radiation-exposed carotid vasculature)
4. Biotene oral gel -- as needed at bedtime and as needed for dry mouth
5. Multivitamin daily
6. Calcium carbonate with vitamin D -- discussed initiating given post-radiation nutritional history

ALLERGIES: NKDA

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, heart rate 68 bpm and regular, respiratory rate 16, temperature 37.0 C, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, weight 70 kg (up approximately 1 kg since last visit; 71 kg at 24 months, weight has been stable in the 69-72 kg range since approximately 12 months post-treatment; pre-treatment weight was approximately 79 kg; she has plateaued at a weight approximately 9-10 kg below her baseline, likely reflecting permanent body composition changes from treatment, though she is not distressed by this).

General: Well-appearing African woman, well-nourished, no acute distress. Alert, oriented, and engaging appropriately. Appears her stated age or younger.

Head: Normocephalic, atraumatic. No temporal wasting. No facial asymmetry.

Eyes: Pupils equal and reactive. Extraocular movements intact. No proptosis.

Ears/Nose/Throat:
- External auditory canals patent bilaterally, no cerumen impaction.
- Oral cavity: Mucosa moist with slightly reduced salivary pooling consistent with chronic xerostomia. No oral ulcerations, leukoplakia, or erythematous mucosal lesions. Dentition intact. Tongue mobile with full range of motion. No induration or firmness of the tongue base on bimanual palpation. Hard and soft palate intact. No trismus apparent at rest; patient reports mouth opening approximately 4.1-4.2 cm with TheraBite exercises.
- Oropharynx: Post-radiation mucosal changes at the right base of tongue and right lateral pharyngeal wall, with smooth, mildly indurated fibrotic mucosa. No visible exophytic or ulcerating lesion. Right tonsillar fossa: absent (post-radiation changes), smooth mucosa. Left tonsil: within normal limits.

Flexible Nasopharyngoscopy (performed in clinic today): Nasal passages clear bilaterally. Nasopharynx: normal mucosa, no mass, no adenoid hypertrophy. Velum, soft palate motion: symmetric and adequate. Oropharynx: right base of tongue with stable, smooth post-radiation fibrotic changes; the area is well-healed with no mucosal irregularity, no ulceration, no suspicious nodularity. Left base of tongue unremarkable. Epiglottis: normal. Vallecula: clear. Arytenoids: symmetric, full mobility bilaterally. True vocal cords: symmetric, adduct fully in midline. Mild posterior commissure erythema consistent with chronic post-radiation laryngeal changes vs. low-grade reflux. No subglottic or supraglottic lesion. Hypopharynx: symmetric, no lesion identified. Piriform sinuses: patent bilaterally.

Neck:
- No palpable cervical, supraclavicular, or submandibular lymphadenopathy. The right neck is notable for soft tissue fibrosis at the radiation field (right lateral neck from the angle of the mandible to the clavicle) with mild firmness and subcutaneous thickening; this is unchanged from prior examinations and does not represent new adenopathy. No tenderness to palpation over the right carotid artery. No audible bruit on auscultation bilaterally.
- No neck lymphedema clinically.

Thyroid: Not enlarged on palpation. No thyroid nodules appreciated on exam.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Shoulder shrug symmetric bilaterally (cranial nerve XI intact, no accessory nerve injury from prior treatment). Tongue protrudes midline. Sensation intact over face and neck. No Horner syndrome.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender. No hepatosplenomegaly. PEG site fully healed, no residual induration.

Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema.

Skin: Radiation field right neck: residual skin changes with mild hyperpigmentation and subtle telangiectasias over the right lateral neck. No moist desquamation. No skin breakdown. No lymphedema of the neck or face.

ECOG Performance Status: 0.

---

LABORATORY AND DIAGNOSTIC RESULTS

Thyroid function (recent, per PCP):
- TSH: 2.1 mIU/L (normal; euthyroid on levothyroxine 100 mcg daily)
- Free T4: within normal limits

Lipid panel (fasting, obtained today for cardiovascular baseline prior to statin initiation):
- Total cholesterol: 198 mg/dL
- LDL cholesterol: 118 mg/dL
- HDL cholesterol: 54 mg/dL
- Triglycerides: 130 mg/dL
- Note: LDL mildly elevated; in the context of prior neck irradiation, cardiovascular risk reduction with statin therapy is indicated per published guidelines regardless of absolute LDL threshold.

Comprehensive metabolic panel (today):
- Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL (GFR > 90 mL/min/1.73m2, normalized after prior cisplatin nephrotoxicity)
- Electrolytes: within normal limits
- Liver function tests: AST, ALT, alkaline phosphatase, total bilirubin all within normal limits
- Albumin: 4.1 g/dL (adequate nutrition)

Complete blood count:
- WBC 6.4 x10^9/L, hemoglobin 13.8 g/dL, platelets 218 x10^9/L (all normal; recovery from treatment-related cytopenias complete)

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Surveillance PET-CT (24 months post-treatment, reviewed at last visit): No FDG-avid disease at the right base of tongue or regional nodes. No distant metastases. Complete ongoing metabolic response.

Thyroid ultrasound (obtained today as part of annual radiation-induced thyroid cancer screening program, first performed today):
- Thyroid gland: mildly heterogeneous echotexture without discrete nodularity. No dominant thyroid nodule identified. No suspicious hypoechoic lesion with irregular margins or microcalcifications. Right thyroid lobe: 3.8 x 1.6 x 1.4 cm. Left thyroid lobe: 3.6 x 1.5 x 1.3 cm. Isthmus: 0.3 cm. No lymphadenopathy in the central compartment.
- Impression: No thyroid nodules. Mildly heterogeneous gland consistent with prior neck irradiation and known hypothyroidism.

Carotid artery duplex Doppler ultrasound (right and left, obtained today -- first carotid screening examination, 30 months post-neck irradiation):
- Right common carotid artery: mild intimal-medial thickening (IMT) at the region corresponding to the radiation field, measuring up to 0.9 mm (upper limit of normal to mildly thickened). Low-level plaque at the right carotid bifurcation, non-flow-limiting. Estimated stenosis < 30% at the right internal carotid artery origin. Peak systolic velocity within the right ICA: 82 cm/s (normal).
- Left common carotid artery: IMT 0.7 mm (within normal limits). No significant plaque. No hemodynamically significant stenosis identified. Peak systolic velocity within the left ICA: 78 cm/s (normal).
- Vertebral arteries: patent, anterograde flow bilaterally.
- Impression: Mild intimal-medial thickening and low-level non-flow-limiting plaque at the right carotid bifurcation, within the prior radiation field. No hemodynamically significant stenosis bilaterally (< 30% stenosis). Findings are consistent with early radiation-accelerated atherosclerosis at the right carotid, as expected given prior high-dose neck irradiation. Recommend annual follow-up carotid ultrasound going forward.

Audiology (from most recent follow-up, approximately 12 months ago):
- Mild high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss at 4000-8000 Hz bilaterally (grade 1 CTCAE). Stable from prior evaluation. Speech discrimination intact. No change from prior audiogram. Likely related to cumulative cisplatin exposure (cumulative dose approximately 414 mg/m2).

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 54-year-old never-smoking woman with HPV-positive right base of tongue squamous cell carcinoma (T2N1M0, p16-positive), treated with definitive concurrent cisplatin-based chemoradiation, who is now 30 months post-treatment with no evidence of disease. She is doing extremely well and continues to demonstrate excellent functional recovery.

1. DISEASE STATUS -- NO EVIDENCE OF DISEASE (NED), 30 MONTHS POST-TREATMENT
   Her clinical examination today is reassuring -- flexible nasopharyngoscopy shows stable post-radiation fibrotic changes at the right base of tongue with no suspicious mucosal lesion, and there is no palpable cervical lymphadenopathy. The 30-month time point is clinically meaningful: in HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC, patients who achieve a durable complete response (particularly those confirmed pathologically, as in Ms. Osei-Bonsu's case) and who remain free of recurrence beyond 24 months have an excellent prognosis, with the vast majority of late relapses uncommon in this biology. We will proceed with continued de-escalated surveillance:
   - Clinical examination every 6 months going forward (per NCCN guidelines for HPV-positive OPSCC, year 3 onward).
   - Next surveillance imaging: annual PET-CT or MRI neck to be obtained at approximately 36 months (3-year mark) post-treatment, per our institutional protocol and NCCN guidance for HPV-positive OPSCC with high-risk features. Given her sustained complete response, we may transition to clinical-only surveillance thereafter if imaging remains clear, per evolving de-escalation data.
   - Patient instructed on symptoms warranting urgent evaluation: new neck mass, worsening dysphagia, hemoptysis, new otalgia, significant voice change. She verbalized understanding.

2. RADIATION-INDUCED CARDIOVASCULAR RISK -- INITIATING STATIN THERAPY
   Carotid Doppler today shows mild intimal-medial thickening and early non-flow-limiting plaque at the right carotid bifurcation within the prior radiation field, consistent with radiation-accelerated atherosclerosis. Baseline LDL is 118 mg/dL, and while this is only mildly elevated by standard cardiovascular guidelines, there is substantial literature supporting aggressive lipid management in patients with prior neck irradiation given the substantially increased lifetime risk of carotid stenosis and cerebrovascular events in this population. Initiating atorvastatin 10 mg daily today. Will titrate to goal LDL < 100 mg/dL at follow-up. Counseled on dietary modifications, regular aerobic exercise. Repeat fasting lipid panel and liver function tests in 3 months. Annual carotid Doppler scheduled.

3. RADIATION-INDUCED HYPOTHYROIDISM -- STABLE
   TSH is well-controlled at 2.1 mIU/L on levothyroxine 100 mcg daily. Thyroid ultrasound today shows no nodules and no suspicious lesion, consistent with post-radiation thyroid changes. No adjustment to levothyroxine dose required. Annual thyroid ultrasound will continue as per our radiation-exposed thyroid surveillance protocol given known increased risk of radiation-induced thyroid nodules and thyroid cancer following neck irradiation; first normal ultrasound today is reassuring. Annual TSH recheck with PCP.

4. XEROSTOMIA -- GRADE 1, STABLE, SLOWLY IMPROVING
   Pilocarpine 5 mg BID continued. Biotene and salivary substitutes continued. She feels this is manageable and is not requesting a dose escalation or alternative sialagogue. Will continue current regimen. Ongoing counseling on dental hygiene given the substantially increased risk of radiation caries in the setting of chronic salivary hypofunction: emphasized daily fluoride toothpaste, water fluoridation, avoidance of cariogenic foods and beverages, and regular dental follow-up every 3-4 months with her dentist. Reinforced importance of dental vigilance and mandibular bone health monitoring (osteoradionecrosis prevention).

5. TRISMUS -- IMPROVING
   Mouth opening approximately 4.1-4.2 cm with TheraBite exercises, improved from approximately 3.8 cm at 12 months post-treatment. She is motivated and compliant with her home program. Will continue TheraBite device daily exercises indefinitely. No intervention required at this time.

6. CISPLATIN-ASSOCIATED HEARING LOSS -- STABLE, GRADE 1
   Mild bilateral high-frequency SNHL stable on prior audiogram. She does not report functional hearing impairment affecting her professional activities as a music teacher. She should alert us if she notices worsening. Repeat audiology in approximately 12 months.

7. LATE DYSPHAGIA -- FUNCTIONALLY ADEQUATE
   Patient is managing a modified-regular diet with minimal limitations. She discontinued formal speech-language pathology sessions and continues her home exercise program. No functional dysphagia requiring reinstitution of SLP care at this time. She will reach out if symptoms worsen or if she has any aspiration events.

8. GENETICS -- BRCA2 VARIANT RECLASSIFIED
   The BRCA2 p.D2723H variant identified on NGS at diagnosis (allele frequency 48%, likely germline) has been reclassified as likely benign by the genetics team, and no further BRCA-specific cascade testing or surveillance imaging beyond standard of care is recommended for Ms. Osei-Bonsu or her family members at this time. She was reassured by this finding and has communicated this to her family. No further genetics follow-up planned unless variant classification changes in the future.

9. PILOCARPINE DOSE
   Pilocarpine was successfully tapered to 5 mg BID from 5 mg TID at 18 months post-treatment. She is tolerating this well. No further dose reduction planned at this time given persistent grade 1 xerostomia.

10. QUALITY OF LIFE / PSYCHOSOCIAL
    Ms. Osei-Bonsu reports excellent quality of life. She returned to full-time work as a music teacher approximately 10 months post-treatment and continues to perform this role without limitation. She denies depressive symptoms or significant anxiety related to cancer fear. She was offered referral to oncologic survivorship support group but declined; she attends a general wellness yoga class through the community. No social work referral required today. She expresses gratitude for her care team and satisfaction with her recovery. HPV vaccination for family members was discussed briefly given the HPV-driven nature of her cancer; she was counseled to discuss with her primary care physician regarding screening and vaccination recommendations for adolescent family members.

FOLLOW-UP:
- Repeat clinical examination in 6 months (36-month post-treatment survivorship visit)
- Fasting lipid panel and LFTs in 3 months (for statin monitoring)
- Annual carotid Doppler ultrasound
- Annual thyroid ultrasound
- Annual TSH
- Annual audiology
- Surveillance imaging at 36-month mark (PET-CT or MRI neck, per protocol)
- Dental evaluation every 3-4 months (per radiation oncology dental protocol)
- Patient to continue TheraBite exercises, pilocarpine, levothyroxine, atorvastatin
- Return precautions: new neck mass, worsening dysphagia, hemoptysis, sudden voice change, neurological symptoms -- presents to emergency department or calls clinic urgently

Time spent with patient: 55 minutes, greater than 50% counseling and coordination of care.

Electronically signed.
Head and Neck Medical Oncology / Survivorship Clinic
